summary: The checked-in repo has a .codex/config.toml but does not enable [features].codex_hooks yet, so packet 026 is still designing a future Codex hook integration rather than extending a live local hook deployment.

Codex loads hooks.json next to active config layers, accumulates matching hooks across files instead of replacing lower-precedence handlers, and currently supports command hooks only because async, prompt, and agent hook handlers are skipped during discovery.

PreToolUse input includes session_id, turn_id, transcript_path, cwd, hook_event_name, model, permission_mode, tool_name, tool_input.command, and tool_use_id, but current matching is Bash-only and the only supported blocking outputs are permissionDecision=deny, legacy decision=block, or exit code 2 with stderr.

PostToolUse gets the same shared metadata plus tool_response, can emit additionalContext, block-style feedback, or continue:false, but all of that happens after the Bash command has already run, so it can redirect model continuation but cannot prevent the side effect that already occurred.

Codex emits HookStarted and HookCompleted events, records HOOK_RUN_METRIC and HOOK_RUN_DURATION_METRIC tagged by hook_name/source/status, and sends analytics HookRunFact payloads with event_name, hook_source, and status.

The platform does not provide dedupe or single-writer guarantees because matching command hooks from multiple files run concurrently and additive discovery can load overlapping policy hooks, so external side effects must self-dedupe using session_id, turn_id, tool_use_id, hook_event_name, and run_id.

Treat Codex UserPromptSubmit as the model-visible advisor surface, PreToolUse as the hard Bash deny path, and PostToolUse as audit or continuation shaping after execution rather than a prevention layer.

Packet 021+ should reuse Codex hook run IDs and native status events for telemetry correlation, and require all external hook side effects to be idempotent because concurrent additive hook execution is part of the platform contract.

Using PreToolUse and PostToolUse alone as a full write-surface enforcement boundary was ruled out because current Codex coverage is Bash-only, shell interception is still incomplete, and non-shell tools remain outside the hook path.
